perturbation.original_text: Each Party will obtain and carry in full force and effect the minimum insurance requirements set forth below. Such insurance (i) will be primary insurance with respect to each Party's own participation under this Agreement and (ii) will be issued by a recognized insurer rated by A.M. Best "A-VII" (or its equivalent) or better, or an insurer pre-approved in writing by the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will obtain and carry insurance. Such insurance (i) may be primary insurance with respect to each Party's own participation under this Agreement and (ii) will be issued by a recognized insurer.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'in full force and effect the minimum insurance requirements set forth below' and replacing it with 'insurance' weakens the clause. Similarly, deleting the A.M. Best rating requirements and the requirement for written pre-approval by the other party reduces the standard required of the insurance coverage, creating legal loopholes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Regulations, Title 10, Section 2695.4
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text mandates a minimum standard of insurance coverage, which aligns with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing required by California law. By weakening this clause, the modified text contradicts 10 CCR § 2695.4, which outlines standards for insurers in handling claims, including requirements for adequate investigation, documentation, and fair settlement practices. The omitted details about insurance requirements create ambiguity and could allow a party to obtain inadequate coverage, increasing the risk to the other party and potentially violating California's insurance regulations. The secondary link provides a general overview of auto insurance requirements, indicating that minimum coverage standards exist.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates minimum insurance requirements, aligning with the duty of good faith. The modified text removes the phrase 'in full force and effect the minimum insurance requirements set forth below' and replaces it with 'insurance.' It also deletes the A.M. Best rating requirements and the requirement for written pre-approval by the other party, which reduces the standard required of the insurance coverage, creating legal loopholes. The omission of specific insurance requirements creates ambiguity and could result in inadequate coverage, potentially violating insurance regulations. The scraped snippets are not helpful, but this is an Omission Legal Contradiction. The original text makes certain obligations regarding the insurance that are now removed, which allows a weaker insurance policy to be used by one of the parties. The modification removes core legal obligations, making the contract less enforceable or compliant.

perturbation.original_text: Within [***] ([***]) days following the Effective Date and on or before the first Business Day of each [***] thereafter, Ginkgo shall furnish BLI with a rolling forecast of the quantities of Beacon Optofluidic Machines (including related Hardware and Software) and Consumables (on a Consumable-by-Consumable basis) that Ginkgo expects to require from BLI under this Agreement during the ensuing [***] ([***]) month period on a [***] basis (each such forecast, a "Rolling Forecast"). Subject to the limitations set forth in this Agreement, Ginkgo may, in its sole discretion, update its estimated requirements of Beacon Optofluidic Machines (including related Hardware and Software) and Consumables in the next Rolling Forecast delivered; provided that (a) the [***] of each Rolling Forecast shall be binding upon the Parties with respect to Beacon Optofluidic Machines and with respect to Consumables, Ginkgo's Purchase Orders for Consumables during the [***] may fluctuate by +/- [***] percent ([***]%) of the amount of Consumables set forth in the Rolling Forecast for the [***]; (b) the [***] of each Rolling Forecast shall include binding amounts forecasted for Beacon Optofluidic Machines and Consumables within (i) [***] percent ([***]%) of the number of units of Beacon Optofluidic Machines or (ii) [***] percent ([***]%) of the number of units of any Consumable; and (c) the [***] of each Rolling Forecast shall be non-binding, good faith estimates of Ginkgo's demand for Beacon Optofluidic Machines and Consumables. The foregoing, along with any other relevant provisions in this Agreement, shall be the sole forecasting mechanics for Ginkgo's requirements of Beacon Optofluidic Machines (including related Hardware and Software) or Consumables expressly notwithstanding anything to the contrary in the BLI Terms and Conditions with respect to forecasting.
perturbation.changed_text: Within [***] ([***]) days following the Effective Date and on or before the first Business Day of each [***] thereafter, Ginkgo shall furnish BLI with a forecast of the quantities of Beacon Optofluidic Machines (including related Hardware and Software) and Consumables (on a Consumable-by-Consumable basis) that Ginkgo expects to require from BLI under this Agreement. Ginkgo may, in its sole discretion, update its estimated requirements of Beacon Optofluidic Machines (including related Hardware and Software) and Consumables in the next forecast delivered.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the specific details on the rolling forecast and the binding nature of certain forecasts, the modified text creates uncertainty around the forecasting process. The removal of clauses (a), (b), and (c) eliminates the requirements for the rolling forecasts to be binding, weakening the agreement's enforcement mechanisms and introducing legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text establishes a clear forecasting mechanism to determine the quantity of goods. By removing the specifics of the forecasts being binding, the modified text violates the UCC § 2-306 requirement for good faith in output and requirements contracts. Without a clear, binding mechanism, the quantity of goods is not determined by good faith, creating ambiguity and potential for disputes. The UCC aims to set a basic standard for sale of goods in a state of the USA and in commercial and economical settings.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith , except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the binding nature of the forecasts, which creates uncertainty in determining the quantity of goods required. According to UCC § 2-306 (Snippet 1), a term that measures quantity by the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith. By removing the specific details of the forecasts being binding, the modified text undermines the 'good faith' requirement because it allows Ginkgo to change its requirements without a clear mechanism or limitations. The original text specified a clear forecasting procedure with binding amounts, ensuring that the quantity of goods was determined in good faith. The omission of these specifics weakens the enforceability of the agreement and introduces the potential for disputes regarding the quantity of goods required, directly contradicting the UCC's intent to set a basic standard for sale of goods.

perturbation.original_text: If any payment due is not paid by the due date, BLI may (a) charge interest on any outstanding amount of such payment, accruing as of the original due date, at an annual rate equal to the rate of prime (as reported in The Wall Street Journal, Eastern U.S. Edition) plus [***] percentage points or the maximum rate allowable by Applicable Law, whichever is less.
perturbation.changed_text: If any payment due is not paid by the due date, BLI may charge interest on any outstanding amount of such payment.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific details related to the interest rate calculation introduces a legal risk. Omitting reference to 'the rate of prime (as reported in The Wall Street Journal, Eastern U.S. Edition) plus [***] percentage points or the maximum rate allowable by Applicable Law, whichever is less' creates ambiguity. It opens the door to disputes regarding what interest rate is 'reasonable' and if this would violate usury laws. Removing these parameters generates legal uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text specifies how the interest rate is calculated, providing clear terms. The modification provides no interest rate calculation, and provides zero protection against violations of usury laws. CA Const. Art. XV § 1 sets limits on interest rates for loans and forbearances. The secondary link provides access to FindLaw's version of this California Constitutional clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the specific interest rate calculation, increasing the risk of violating usury laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing the California Constitution Article XV, sets limits on interest rates. The original text limits the interest rate to the prime rate plus a percentage or the maximum allowable by law. Removing this exposes BLI to potential violations of usury laws because no interest rate cap is established, contradicting the law's intent to limit interest rates.
